smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a 20-year-old handcrafted dip, soup, and seasoning brand with great flavors and loyal fans but no ads running and almost no reviews on-site, so this call is about starting from zero the smart way, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand founded by Ellie Hughes in 2005. They sell dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs, and seasonings direct to consumers online and also do wholesale and fundraisers. Based in Wichita, KS and running on Shopify.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: 103 products. Dry mixes you turn into dips, soups, and rubs at home. Prices run from $5.50 to $48.99, with a median around $9.99. They also sell multi-packs (4-packs at $22.99, 3-packs at $29 to $36) and gift boxes, which is how they lift order size.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Gluten free, no added MSG, low sodium. The hook is bold flavor without the fuss.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold, gourmet flavor fast, plus gift-givers buying for the holidays and for food-loving friends and family.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays.
Jalapeno Lime Rub is absolutely amazing. I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time.
The Enchilada Sauce Seasoning is so authentic, I don't have to make my Grandmother's Recipe.
strengths: Real, specific flavors people say they are addicted to and reorder. The customer quotes show genuine repeat love, which is gold for ad copy.
Multi-packs and gift boxes already exist, so there is a clear path to lift order value above the low single-product price.
gaps: No reviews show on the product pages (the 4-pack pages literally say 'Be the first to write a review'). No visible social proof makes cold ads harder to land.

No Meta ads appear to be running, so there is no proven winning ad or audience to build on. We start from scratch. Also the shipping policy page just says 'Coming Soon', which is a trust gap at checkout.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero with one proven-love hero product, prove one ad can sell profitably, then widen the audience and add a second product only after we have a winner.

phases.goal: Prove that one ad can sell one product profitably to cold buyers. We are not chasing scale, we are finding the first thing that works.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign on Meta, broad targeting, letting Meta find the buyers.
A basic retargeting campaign so people who click but don't buy see the product again.
lead_products: Lead with the Jalapeno Lime Rub. A real customer review calls it 'absolutely amazing' and lists five different ways she uses it, which is the kind of specific, believable love that makes a first ad work. If the account or product photos aren't ad-ready for it, fall back to the Uptown BBQ Rub ($10.49), since the homepage is already leaning into 'It's BBQ Time' and the season is right.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet we already sent. Put the strongest one live first: the one built around the hero rub and the 'use it on everything' angle, because it matches how their real customers actually talk (steak, eggs, avocado toast, bacon). Lean hard on the free shipping over $40 plus free gift offer to push people toward a multi-pack.
phases.budget: Start at $30 to $50 a day. Enough to learn, small enough that a brand new to ads won't panic.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can point to a handful of sales that clearly came from the ad, and at least one ad and one audience that look worth more money. Not a full-blown profit machine yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and audience with a higher daily budget.
Add 2 to 3 fresh versions of the winning ad to keep it from going stale.
Turn on a simple offer to lift order size, pushing the 4-packs and gift bundles.
lead_products: Keep the month-one winner as the hero. Introduce the matching 4-pack (for example the 4 Pack I Like it Spicy or Veggies & Chiles at $22.99) as the upsell, since that gets people over the $40 free shipping line.
phases.creative: Add short customer-quote ads using the real reviews we have (the 'addicted to your soups and dips' quote, the Jalapeno Lime Rub quote). This is also when we start collecting more reviews to fix the social proof gap.
phases.budget: Step up to $75 to $125 a day, only if month one earned it. If it didn't, we fix the ad or offer before we spend more.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A steady daily flow of orders from ads at a return we both agree is healthy, plus a rising average order value from the multi-packs.

phases.goal: Scale the proven play and add a second product so we are not resting on one hero.
campaigns: Launch a second product campaign, most likely a soup mix, timed for cooler weather.
Build a gift-focused campaign to start warming up the holiday shopper, since their own customers say they buy for gifts.
lead_products: Add a soup mix as product two, likely the Baked Potato soup (their own site calls it 'Best Seller') inside the Comfort Soup Collection 3-pack at $30. Soup sells into fall and the 3-pack clears the free shipping line on its own.
phases.creative: Gift-angle ads built around the Gift Boxes and 3-pack collections, plus 'Soup's On in 30 minutes' for the soup push. Reuse the winning ad structure from the rub, just with the new product.
phases.budget: Scale based on what the numbers support. We raise budget in steps, not jumps.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling from ads, a real audience that keeps working, and a gift campaign lined up before the holiday rush.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 20 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in the food and specialty grocery category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience work before we spend more

estimates.label: Order size we are working with
estimates.value: About $10 on a single item, which is why we push toward $40
estimates.basis: their catalog median price is about $9.99, and they already offer free shipping plus a free gift at $40, so the whole plan leans on multi-packs and gift boxes to get orders up to a size that can carry paid ads

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We can't put an honest dollar figure on this before the call
estimates.basis: we have no access to their store, so we don't know their real average order size, their margin, or their conversion rate. Kyle should get those three numbers on the call, then we can size a target. Guessing here would be a mistake.

assumptions: That their margin on a $10 mix is healthy enough to afford paid ads once order size is lifted toward $40. If the margin is thin, the whole plan needs a higher starting price point.
That the Jalapeno Lime Rub is real, in stock, and has usable product photos. If not, we lead with the Uptown BBQ Rub.
That the site actually converts visitors into buyers today. If the site is leaky, ads will just pour money into a bucket with a hole, and we fix the site first.
That the 'Shipping policy: Coming Soon' page is an oversight and shipping actually works. This needs confirming before we send cold traffic.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else? And have you ever?
questions.listening_for: We found no ads running. Confirm whether they never started or tried and stopped. If they stopped, why they stopped is the most important thing you learn all call.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today, you or someone on your team?
questions.listening_for: If Ellie does it all herself, she is time-poor and wants it off her plate. If there's a team member, find out how much freedom they'd have to work with us.

questions.question: When someone buys a single mix for around ten dollars, roughly how much of that is profit after you make it and ship it?
questions.listening_for: This decides if ads are even viable. A ten dollar item with thin margin can't carry ad costs alone. If margin is tight, we must lean on 4-packs and gift boxes from day one.

questions.question: What does a typical order look like, one item or usually a few?
questions.listening_for: Their real average order size. We are guessing around ten dollars from list prices, but if most people already buy multi-packs, the numbers look much better and we can start bolder.

questions.question: Where do most of your sales come from today, the website, farmers markets, wholesale, or events?
questions.listening_for: The site mentions Locations, Events, and Wholesale. If they are mostly an in-person and wholesale business, the website may not be built to convert cold online buyers yet, and that changes month one.

questions.question: Out of everything you sell, what are your two or three biggest sellers, and who buys them?
questions.listening_for: Confirm or replace our hero pick. We are betting on the Jalapeno Lime Rub because of the review, but Ellie knows her real top sellers. Let her data beat our guess.

questions.question: I noticed your product pages have almost no reviews showing. Do you collect reviews anywhere, like email or in person?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is a pile of happy feedback we can put on the site and in ads. No social proof is a real handicap for cold ads, so any reviews they can share are valuable.

questions.question: Your shipping policy page says 'Coming Soon' right now. What actually happens when someone orders, how fast do you ship and what does it cost them?
questions.listening_for: A trust and checkout gap. If shipping info is missing or unclear, people abandon carts. We may need this fixed before ads go live.

questions.question: When you sell in person, what makes people say yes? What do they taste and love?
questions.listening_for: The exact words and flavors that close sales face to face. That is our best ad copy, straight from real buyers.

questions.question: Do people ever find you by searching Google for things like 'gluten free dip mix' or 'homemade soup mix'?
questions.listening_for: There is a real opening here. People search for terms like 'dip mix', 'taco seasoning', and 'BBQ rub' by name. If they say yes or don't know, that is a door to Google Search ads later, once Meta is working.

questions.question: Do you do much for the holidays? Your customers say they buy your stuff as gifts.
questions.listening_for: Their own review says the products get bought for gifts over the holidays. That is a natural fit for gift-keyword search ads in Q4 and a reason to plan ahead.

objections.objection: My products are only ten dollars, I can't make money paying for ads.
objections.answer: You're right that a single ten dollar mix can't carry ads alone, and that's exactly why the whole plan pushes people toward your 4-packs, gift boxes, and your own free shipping over forty dollars offer. We aim to get the order size up, not just sell one jar. That's the difference between ads that lose money and ads that work here.

objections.objection: I've never run ads, isn't this risky and expensive?
objections.answer: That's why we start at thirty to fifty dollars a day, not hundreds. Month one is just to prove one ad can sell one product. We don't spend more until something is actually working, and you'll see exactly what's happening the whole way.

objections.objection: I don't have many reviews or fancy photos, so what would we even run?
objections.answer: We already built you three ads from your own products, and they're ready. Your real customers gave us gold, like the woman who uses your Jalapeno Lime Rub on five different things. We use that. And getting more reviews on your site is built into month two, so we fix that gap as we go.

objections.objection: I sell a lot at markets and wholesale, is online really worth the effort?
objections.answer: Your in-person sales prove people love the flavor once they taste it. Ads let more people find you when you're not standing at a booth, and they run all year, including the holidays when your own customers already buy your stuff as gifts. We're just widening a door that already works.

red_flags.flag: Very low single-item price, around ten dollars median.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At that price a single sale can't cover ad costs plus profit. The whole plan depends on lifting order size with multi-packs and the forty dollar free shipping line. If Ellie resists that, ads will struggle.

red_flags.flag: No reviews showing on product pages.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold buyers trust other buyers. With no visible social proof, first-time visitors from ads are more likely to bounce. We need to get reviews onto the site early.
Missing shipping info at checkout kills trust and causes abandoned carts. Sending paid traffic to a store with unclear shipping wastes money. Confirm this is just an oversight before ads go live.

red_flags.flag: No ads currently running and possibly no ad experience.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We have no past winner to build on and Ellie may have never seen how ads perform. Set expectations that month one is learning, not scaling, or she may pull the plug too early.
Too many choices can scatter focus and spend. We must pick one hero product and hold the line, or the account gets messy and nothing gets a fair test.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads: people actively search for terms like 'gluten free dip mix', 'taco seasoning mix', and 'BBQ rub', which matches their catalog directly. This is a real opening for later, once Meta is proven.
Holiday and gift search: their own customer review says products get bought as gifts over the holidays, so gift-related searches in Q4 are worth pursuing.

SEO on their recipe pages: they already publish recipes (Spinach Artichoke, Lentil Soup, Pea Soup). Recipe content is something people search for and could pull in free traffic over time, so it's a fair SEO conversation, though it pays off slower than ads.
